era_true.statement: Copper is a better electrical conductor than iron.
A half-wave dipole antenna has a length equal to half the wavelength of the operating frequency.
Vacuum tubes amplify electrical signals by controlling electron flow through a heated cathode.
The superheterodyne receiver converts incoming signals to a fixed intermediate frequency before detection.
Frequency modulation provides better noise rejection than amplitude modulation for radio broadcasting.
A triode vacuum tube contains a cathode, a grid, and a plate.
Impedance matching between an antenna and a transmission line maximizes power transfer.
The resonant frequency of an LC circuit equals one divided by two pi times the square root of LC.
Armstrong invented the regenerative circuit and the superheterodyne receiver.
A pentode tube has three grids: the control grid, the screen grid, and the suppressor grid.
Higher frequencies propagate more by line of sight than by ground wave.
The ionosphere reflects shortwave radio signals back to Earth.
Ohm's law states that voltage equals current multiplied by resistance.
A capacitor stores energy in an electric field between two conductive plates.
An inductor stores energy in a magnetic field created by current flowing through a coil.
AM radio broadcasting in the United States uses the frequency band from 540 to 1600 kilocycles.
FM radio broadcasting operates in the very high frequency band around 88 to 108 megacycles.
A rectifier converts alternating current into direct current.
The diode vacuum tube allows current to flow in only one direction.
Kirchhoff's current law states that the total current entering a junction equals the total current leaving it.
A transformer changes voltage levels through electromagnetic induction between two coils.
Skin effect causes high-frequency current to flow near the surface of a conductor.
A Yagi antenna uses a driven element, a reflector, and one or more directors.
The characteristic impedance of free space is 377 ohms.
Grid bias voltage controls the operating point of a vacuum tube amplifier.
Negative feedback in an amplifier reduces distortion and increases bandwidth.
The bandwidth of an AM signal is twice the highest modulating frequency.
A crystal oscillator uses a piezoelectric quartz crystal to generate a stable frequency.
Coaxial cable shields the inner conductor from external electromagnetic interference.
The Q factor of a resonant circuit describes its selectivity and energy storage efficiency.
Electromagnetic waves travel at the speed of light in a vacuum.
A balanced transmission line has two conductors carrying equal and opposite currents.
Plate dissipation is the power lost as heat in the anode of a vacuum tube.
The decibel is a logarithmic unit used to express power ratios.
A class A amplifier conducts current through the full 360 degrees of the input cycle.
A class B amplifier conducts current through 180 degrees of the input cycle.
Heterodyning produces sum and difference frequencies from two input signals.
The magnetron tube generates microwave energy and was used in radar during World War II.
Standing waves on a transmission line result from impedance mismatch at the load.
An antenna's radiation pattern describes the directional distribution of radiated energy.
The Earth's circumference at the equator is roughly 25,000 miles.
Water freezes at 32 degrees Fahrenheit at standard atmospheric pressure.
The speed of light in a vacuum is 186,000 miles per second.
The Moon orbits the Earth with a period of about 27.3 days.
The Pacific Ocean is the largest ocean on Earth.
Mount Everest is the tallest mountain above sea level on Earth.
The Nile River is the longest river in Africa.
The Amazon River carries more water by volume than any other river on Earth.
The Earth is the third planet from the Sun.
Light from the Sun takes about eight minutes to reach the Earth.
The Sahara Desert is located in northern Africa.
Sound travels faster through water than through air.
Hydrogen is the lightest element on the periodic table.
Gold is denser than silver.
The boiling point of water at sea level is 212 degrees Fahrenheit.
Mercury is the only metal that is liquid at room temperature.
The Earth rotates on its axis once every 24 hours.
The human heart pumps blood through the circulatory system.
Nitrogen makes up the largest portion of Earth's atmosphere by volume.
The Great Wall of China was built over many centuries to defend against northern invasions.
The Panama Canal connects the Atlantic Ocean to the Pacific Ocean.
The Suez Canal connects the Mediterranean Sea to the Red Sea.
The United States declared independence from Great Britain in 1776.
The French Revolution began in 1789.
World War I lasted from 1914 to 1918.
World War II lasted from 1939 to 1945.
The atomic bomb was first used in warfare against Japan in August 1945.
The United Nations was established in 1945.
Germany surrendered to the Allied forces in May 1945.
Japan surrendered to the Allied forces in August 1945.
The Wright brothers achieved powered heavier-than-air flight in 1903.
Alexander Graham Bell patented the telephone in 1876.
Guglielmo Marconi demonstrated long-distance radio communication in 1901.
The Titanic sank in the North Atlantic Ocean in April 1912.
The Great Depression began with the stock market crash of 1929.
Penicillin was discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1928.
The Soviet Union was established in 1922.
The Treaty of Versailles was signed in 1919 ending World War I.
Pearl Harbor was attacked by Japan on December 7, 1941.
D-Day, the Allied invasion of Normandy, occurred on June 6, 1944.
The League of Nations was formed after World War I.
The Hindenburg airship disaster occurred in 1937 in New Jersey.
Charles Lindbergh flew solo across the Atlantic Ocean in 1927.
The speed of sound in air at sea level is about 1,100 feet per second.
Radar was used extensively by both Allied and Axis powers during World War II.
London is the capital of the United Kingdom.
Paris is the capital of France.
Moscow is the capital of the Soviet Union.
Washington, D.C. is the capital of the United States.
The Mississippi River is the longest river in the United States.
Australia is both a country and a continent.
Brazil is the largest country in South America by area.
Canada borders the United States to the north.
India gained independence from British rule in 1947.
The state of Israel was established in 1948.
The Berlin blockade by the Soviet Union occurred in 1948 and 1949.
Alaska became the 49th state of the United States in 1949.
The North Atlantic Treaty Organization was established in 1949.
China became a communist state under Mao Zedong in 1949.
The electron was discovered by J.J. Thomson in 1897.
Albert Einstein published the theory of special relativity in 1905.
Electrons carry a negative electrical charge.
Protons carry a positive electrical charge.
The neutron was discovered by James Chadwick in 1932.
Uranium is a radioactive element used in nuclear fission.
The periodic table organizes elements by atomic number.
An electric current flowing through a wire creates a magnetic field around the wire.
James Clerk Maxwell formulated the classical theory of electromagnetic radiation.
Heinrich Hertz experimentally confirmed the existence of electromagnetic waves in 1887.
The vacuum tube was developed from early work by Fleming and De Forest.
Lee De Forest invented the Audion triode tube in 1906.
The British Broadcasting Corporation began radio transmissions in the 1920s.
The Federal Communications Commission regulates radio broadcasting in the United States.
A galvanometer measures small electrical currents.
Solder made from tin and lead is used to join electrical connections.
The cathode ray tube displays images by directing an electron beam onto a phosphorescent screen.
Morse code uses patterns of short and long signals to represent letters and numbers.
The telegraph preceded the telephone as a long-distance electrical communication system.
Shortwave radio signals can travel thousands of miles by reflecting off the ionosphere.
A waveguide is a hollow metallic tube used to transmit microwave energy.

era_false.statement: Radio waves travel faster in a vacuum than light does.
Guglielmo Marconi's wireless apparatus operated by transmitting longitudinal sound waves rather than electromagnetic waves.
A capacitor stores energy in a magnetic field.
The first transatlantic radio transmission occurred in 1925.
Ohm's Law states that voltage equals current divided by resistance.
Nikola Tesla invented the vacuum tube triode.
Frequency is measured in units called farads.
AM radio broadcasting began commercially in the United States in 1945.
Inductors oppose changes in voltage, not current.
Heinrich Hertz demonstrated radio waves in 1920.
A transformer can operate on direct current with no modifications.
The speed of radio wave propagation is approximately 3,000 meters per second.
Lee de Forest invented the simple diode vacuum tube.
Shortwave radio signals cannot travel beyond line of sight.
Resistance in a circuit is measured in henrys.
The BBC began radio broadcasting in 1898.
A series RLC circuit at resonance has maximum impedance.
Edwin Armstrong invented amplitude modulation.
Radio waves and X-rays are entirely different physical phenomena rather than both being electromagnetic radiation.
The superheterodyne receiver was invented by Thomas Edison.
Wavelength and frequency are directly proportional for electromagnetic waves.
Copper is a poor conductor of electricity compared to rubber.
The ionosphere absorbs all radio waves and prevents long-distance communication.
World War II ended in 1943.
A full-wave rectifier uses only one diode.
The electromagnetic spectrum places radio waves at higher frequencies than X-rays.
Kirchhoff's voltage law states that voltages around a closed loop sum to the supply voltage.
The first commercial FM radio station began broadcasting in 1890.
Alternating current was pioneered by Thomas Edison as his preferred system.
A quarter-wave antenna for a 1 MHz signal is approximately 300 meters long.
Vacuum tubes were first developed in the 1930s by RCA engineers.
In a parallel circuit, the total resistance is always greater than the largest individual resistance.
The crystal radio receiver requires an external power source to operate.
James Clerk Maxwell published his electromagnetic theory in 1910.
Increasing the plate voltage in a triode decreases the plate current.
Germany banned all radio broadcasting throughout the 1930s.
The characteristic impedance of free space is approximately 37 ohms.
Amplitude modulation was invented in 1942 by the United States military.
A half-wave dipole antenna has an impedance of approximately 300 ohms.
Electrons flow from the plate to the cathode inside a vacuum tube.
The Morse code signal for SOS is three dashes, three dots, three dashes.
Radar technology was first developed after World War II ended.
Power in a DC circuit equals current multiplied by resistance.
The Federal Communications Commission was established in 1901.
Skin effect causes high-frequency current to flow through the center of a conductor.
Michael Faraday discovered electromagnetic induction in 1895.
A low-pass filter blocks all frequencies below its cutoff frequency.
The first radio distress signal from a ship was sent in 1935.
Reactance in a purely resistive circuit varies with frequency.
RCA was founded in France in 1905.
The grid in a triode vacuum tube controls current by varying the filament temperature.
Electromagnetic waves were first predicted by Isaac Newton.
A step-up transformer decreases voltage while increasing current.
The standard intermediate frequency for AM superheterodyne receivers is 10 MHz.
Sound waves and radio waves travel at the same speed.
The pentode vacuum tube contains three electrodes.
Bandwidth of an AM signal is equal to the modulating frequency.
Alexander Graham Bell invented the radio.
Magnetic flux is measured in amperes.
The RMS value of a sine wave equals its peak value.
Coaxial cable was invented in 1948 by Bell Laboratories.
Adding a capacitor in series with an inductor at resonance creates infinite current in a real circuit.
The United States entered World War I in 1920.
A galvanometer measures capacitance.
Radio wave diffraction was disproven by experiments in the 1930s.
The cathode ray tube was invented by Guglielmo Marconi.
In a vacuum tube, the screen grid was added to increase Miller capacitance.
Frequency modulation produces a signal with constant amplitude and narrower bandwidth than AM.
The Titanic sank in 1918 in the Pacific Ocean.
Impedance is the simple sum of resistance and reactance.
The first vacuum tube computer was built in the 1800s.
A dipole antenna radiates most strongly along its axis.
The piezoelectric effect was discovered by Albert Einstein.
A carbon resistor dissipates no heat when direct current flows through it.
Coulomb's law states that electric force increases with distance between charges.
The magnetron was invented specifically for radio broadcasting purposes.
Phase modulation and amplitude modulation are identical processes.
The speed of light was first measured by Nikola Tesla.
An oscilloscope measures resistance directly.
The Battle of Britain took place in 1939 over the Atlantic Ocean.
Ground wave propagation is most effective at very high frequencies above 300 MHz.
A Wheatstone bridge circuit is used to measure inductance only.
The electron was discovered by Heinrich Hertz in 1905.
Negative feedback in an amplifier increases distortion and narrows bandwidth.
Pearl Harbor was attacked on December 7, 1940.
The decibel unit was named after Alexander Graham Bell and measures voltage directly.
Thermal noise in resistors decreases as temperature increases.
The cavity magnetron was developed in Italy during World War I.
A transmission line with a standing wave ratio of one has maximum signal reflection.
Bakelite is an excellent conductor of electricity widely used for wiring.
The pentagrid converter tube contains three grids.
Antenna gain violates conservation of energy by creating additional power.
The first patent for radio was granted to Samuel Morse.
Eddy currents in transformer cores improve efficiency.
The Heaviside layer was proposed by Thomas Edison in 1880.
Increasing antenna height decreases its effective range for line-of-sight communication.
The standard broadcast AM band in the United States spans 10 to 50 MHz.
A class C amplifier has the highest linearity among amplifier classes.
The standard intermediate frequency for FM receivers is lower than that used for AM receivers.
Mutual inductance between two coils decreases when they are brought closer together.
The cathode in a vacuum tube is always cold and unheated.
A vacuum diode conducts current equally well in both directions.
The Q factor of a resonant circuit decreases as losses decrease.
Signal propagation delay in cables is independent of the cable's dielectric material.
World War II began when France invaded Germany in 1942.
A Yagi antenna uses only a single element for directional reception.
The photoelectric effect was explained by Niels Bohr using wave theory.
Voltage standing wave ratio applies only to audio frequency circuits.
The first television broadcast occurred in Germany in 1895.
Lenz's law states that induced current flows in the same direction as the change causing it.
Tungsten is used for vacuum tube filaments because of its low melting point.
The wavelength of a 100 megacycle signal in free space is about 30 centimeters.
A bridge rectifier requires a center-tapped transformer to function.
The characteristic impedance of standard coaxial cable is approximately 5 ohms.
Superheterodyne receivers were first used commercially in the 1940s.
An electromagnet becomes weaker when more current flows through its coil.
The first patent for FM radio was awarded to David Sarnoff.
Dielectric breakdown occurs when an insulator becomes a better insulator under high voltage.
The waveguide was theoretically impossible according to Maxwell's equations.
Armstrong's regenerative circuit decreased receiver sensitivity compared to earlier designs.

era_believed.statement: The vacuum tube is the permanent foundation of electronic amplification and computing, and any future electronic apparatus will be built upon thermionic valves.
The maximum usable frequency for reliable long-distance radio communication cannot exceed roughly thirty megacycles, because higher frequencies pierce the ionosphere and are lost to space.
Frequencies in the very high and ultra high ranges are useful only for short line-of-sight links and have no value for long-distance communication.
The Kennelly-Heaviside layer is a single sharply defined reflecting surface that returns radio waves to Earth like a mirror at a fixed height.
Atmospheric static is a fundamental and permanent limitation on radio reception that no receiver design can overcome.
Radio waves above the shortwave bands cannot be reflected by the upper atmosphere and are therefore useless for sky-wave communication.
The ionosphere forms an impenetrable shell, so radio waves can never escape the atmosphere to reach the space beyond it.
A heated cathode is essential to every amplifying device, since useful amplification of an electrical signal demands the emission of electrons from a hot surface.
Germanium and similar crystal rectifiers are suitable only as simple detectors and can never serve as practical amplifiers.
The frequency stability of a quartz crystal oscillator is fixed once and for all by the cut of the crystal and cannot be improved by the surrounding circuitry.
The efficiency of a radio transmitter is limited chiefly by the resistance of its tuning coils, and dielectric losses in capacitors may be neglected entirely.
The resistance of every metallic conductor falls steadily toward zero as it is cooled, reaching zero at the absolute zero of temperature.
Cosmic interference and galactic noise are negligible at radio frequencies, so the only fundamental limit to reception is the thermal agitation within the receiver itself.
Television pictures cannot be transmitted over distances greater than the optical horizon under any circumstances.
The picture detail of a television image is limited absolutely by the number of scanning lines and can never approach the sharpness of a photograph.
The capacity of a transmission line to carry intelligence is fixed solely by its physical dimensions and is wholly independent of the noise present.
The amount of information that a communication channel can convey has no fundamental limit and may be increased without bound by refining the apparatus.
The human ear cannot distinguish musical reproduction above about eight thousand cycles, so transmitting higher audio frequencies in broadcasting is pointless.
Reception of stations beyond the horizon on the very short waves is impossible, since such waves travel only in straight lines and are never scattered by the troposphere.
Television will always require the viewer to sit in a darkened room, for no picture tube can be made bright enough to be watched in daylight.
The transistor is a fragile laboratory curiosity that will never displace the vacuum tube in the practical radio receiver or transmitter.
Point-contact germanium devices are far too noisy and unstable ever to serve as the sensitive front end of a communications receiver.
No amplifying device can ever be built without a heated emitter, for the liberation of electrons from a cold solid in useful quantity is a physical impossibility.
Colour television is an impractical dream that can never be reduced to a broadcast service the ordinary household will receive.
Speech and music can never be carried across the ocean by a submarine cable, and the long radio circuit must always remain the only link between the continents.
An electronic computing machine must always fill a great room and consume the power of a small factory, so such engines will remain rare instruments of the laboratory.
Printed wiring laid down as a metallic film upon an insulating board can never carry the currents of a transmitter and is fit only for cheap toys.
Beyond the centimetre waves there lies no useful radio region whatever, for no apparatus could ever be made to generate or detect millimetre waves.
The reception of a signal reflected from the Moon must remain forever beyond reach, for no transmitter on Earth could return a detectable echo across so vast a distance.
A repeating station hung permanently above the Earth to relay radio signals belongs wholly to the realm of fancy and can never be built.
Magnetic recording upon tape or wire can never approach the disc for fidelity and is suited only to the dictation of letters and the rough notes of the amateur.
Frequency modulation, demanding so wide a channel, is wasteful of the spectrum and can never be accommodated in the crowded broadcast bands.
A loaded aerial only a few feet long fed against a good earth radiates as strongly as a full half-wave aerial cut for the same frequency.
The fading of a distant short-wave station is wholly a daily and seasonal matter, never varying from minute to minute, so a steady signal may always be had by choosing the right hour.
Tropospheric conditions have no bearing whatever upon the propagation of the ultra-short waves, whose range is fixed once and for all by the height of the aerials.
The single sideband is a mere curiosity of the laboratory, and the double-sideband transmission of the broadcast station will always remain the only sound practice.
The strength of a broadcast signal at a distant point can be predicted exactly from the transmitter power and the distance alone, the nature of the intervening ground being of no account.
Once a superheterodyne is provided with a sufficient number of intermediate-frequency stages its sensitivity may be raised without any limit set by noise.
The pulse methods of radar can never be applied to the conveyance of speech or music, which must always be carried by a continuous modulated wave.
Standing waves upon an aerial feeder are wholly without harm and may be left out of account altogether in the design of a transmitting station.
A directional aerial array can be made sharply directive only at the shortest wavelengths, so no useful beam can be formed in the broadcast band by any arrangement of wires.
The amplification a single valve can yield is fixed by the valve alone, and no arrangement of the circuit about it can ever increase the gain to be had from one tube.
Atmospheric noise sets the same severe limit upon reception in the ultra-short waves as it does in the medium broadcast band.
The ground wave of a broadcast station travels farther over dry sandy soil than over the sea or over moist and fertile ground.
The directivity of a parabolic reflector grows without any limit as the wavelength is shortened, no bound being set by the size of the dish.
Automatic frequency control is a needless refinement, for a well-made local oscillator never drifts once the receiver has thoroughly warmed.
A tuned radio-frequency receiver of sufficient stages will always out-perform a superheterodyne, which only adds to its faults the defect of image response.
The crystal mixer of a centimetre-wave receiver converts purely by its rectifying contact and adds no noise of its own to the signal it receives.
The whistling note heard as a regenerative receiver approaches oscillation arrives already within the incoming signal and is in no way created within the set itself.
A loud-speaker of the moving-coil pattern can never reproduce notes below about a hundred cycles, so the lower octaves of music are lost to broadcasting forever.
Selenium and copper-oxide rectifiers are crude makeshifts fit only for the charging of accumulators and can never rival the vacuum diode in any radio service.
The cavity magnetron generates its microwaves upon the very same principle as an ordinary triode oscillator and marks no real advance over it.
The depth of modulation of a carrier can never be carried safely beyond about half, for any greater depth must over-drive the transmitter and produce a distorted signal.
The image response of a superheterodyne can be done away with altogether by choosing the intermediate frequency low enough.
Negative feedback carried around an amplifier is certain in the end to throw it into oscillation and is therefore unfit for high-quality reproduction.
A neutralising condenser cancels the grid-to-plate capacitance of a valve at every frequency alike, and not merely at the one frequency for which it is adjusted.
The mutual conductance of a pentode may be raised without any limit merely by drawing its grid wires closer together, with no penalty in any other quantity.
The plate resistance of a triode is a fixed property of the tube itself and does not alter with the operating point chosen for the circuit.
Two stations sharing the same channel can never both be received, for the stronger must wholly suppress the weaker by the mere fact of its greater field.
The fidelity of a disc recording is limited only by the fineness of the cutting stylus and never by the slow speed of the groove at the inner diameter.
Iron-cored chokes are without value at radio frequencies, for iron will carry no magnetic flux once the frequency has risen above the audible range.
The selectivity of a receiver may be sharpened without any limit merely by adding further tuned circuits, however close the wanted and unwanted stations may lie.
A pentode is wholly free of distortion, the curvature of its characteristic having been removed by the addition of the screen and suppressor grids.
The carbon-granule microphone is the most faithful transducer of speech to be had and cannot be bettered for either telephone or broadcast service.
A class C amplifier may be used to amplify a speech-modulated carrier directly without introducing any distortion of the envelope.
The dielectric chosen for a transmitting condenser has no bearing upon its losses, which are governed wholly by the area and spacing of its plates.
The radiation from a half-wave aerial is the same in every direction at right angles to the wire, the pattern being a perfect circle without any nulls.
A receiver may be made as sensitive as one pleases simply by adding stage upon stage of high-frequency amplification ahead of the detector.
The power that a broadcast transmitter must employ to cover a given service area is the same by day as by night, the ionosphere having no influence upon the ground wave.
Frequencies above three hundred megacycles can carry no intelligence of any kind and lie forever beyond the useful spectrum.
An undamped continuous wave can convey nothing but the telegraph dot and dash, for a pure carrier cannot be made to carry the voice.
The piezoelectric crystal is useful only as a fixed standard of frequency and can play no part in the filtering or selection of a band of signals.
Wide-band amplification is impossible, for any valve circuit made to amplify a broad band of frequencies must sacrifice all of its gain in doing so.
The capacity of a condenser may be made large only by a great area of metal foil, no chemical process being able to win a large capacitance from a small volume.
The losses in a coaxial line arise almost wholly from the resistance of its copper conductors, the dielectric between them contributing nothing worth reckoning.
Ferrite and powdered-iron cores are far too lossy for service above the broadcast band and must be confined to audio and power transformers.
The bandwidth a channel needs is set only by the highest note to be conveyed and bears no relation to the speed at which signals may be sent through it.
A superheterodyne can be tuned across a wide band without any tracking error, the signal and oscillator circuits keeping step of their own accord.
The noise figure of a receiver is of no consequence in long-distance work, where atmospheric static alone determines what may be heard.
Quartz crystals can be ground to oscillate only up to a few hundred kilocycles, so no crystal control of frequency is possible in the short-wave bands.
The whip aerial of a motor-car radiates with equal strength in every direction about the horizon, the metal body beneath it having no effect upon the pattern.
A short vertical aerial worked against earth is so poor a radiator that it can never put a useful signal into the ionosphere for long-distance work.
The skin effect ceases altogether once a conductor has been plated with silver, after which the current flows evenly throughout the whole of its cross-section.
The energy of the magnetic field of a choke vanishes the instant its current is broken, so the interruption of an inductive circuit gives rise to no spark.
The earth connection of a transmitting station serves only to guard the apparatus against lightning and takes no part in the radiation of the signal.
A grounded grid amplifier can give no power gain whatever and is therefore of no use save as a buffer between two stages.
The reactance offered by a coil is the same at every frequency and depends only upon the number of its turns.
A condenser placed across the terminals of a steady battery passes a continuous direct current for so long as the connection is maintained.
Increasing the height of a transmitting aerial can do nothing to extend the range of a ground-wave signal over the curved Earth.
The intelligence carried by a modulated wave resides wholly in its carrier, the sidebands being a mere by-product that may be discarded without loss.
A push-pull output stage produces twice the harmonic distortion of a single valve, since two tubes must distort more than one.
The decibel measures the ratio of two currents directly, without any regard to the impedances in which those currents flow.
The grid of a power valve may be driven as hard as one pleases without affecting the purity of the output, distortion being governed by the plate voltage alone.
Wire recording upon steel wire gives a reproduction superior to any tape and will remain the standard for the permanent storage of sound.
The selenium photo-cell follows changes of illumination without any lag, so a televised scene may be scanned point by point with no smearing of the image.
A quarter-wave stub bridged across a feeder presents a dead short to the line at every frequency alike.
The velocity of a wave along a coaxial cable is the free-space velocity divided by the dielectric constant rather than by its square root.
Beyond the few channels already crowded into the very high frequencies there can be no further room for broadcasting, the spectrum above being barren of use.
The fidelity of broadcast reproduction can never be carried beyond that of the telephone, for the ear demands no more and the channel can give no more.
The reliability of any complex electronic apparatus is governed chiefly by the number of its valves, and a set of a hundred tubes can never be made to run for long without failure.
A frequency-modulated transmitter radiates upon a single fixed frequency and so occupies no wider a channel than the unmodulated carrier alone.
The directivity of an aerial array can be improved only by lengthening its elements, never by increasing the number of elements alone.
The detector of a broadcast receiver introduces no distortion of its own and reproduces the modulation envelope with perfect faithfulness.
A long aerial gathers more signal than a short one in strict proportion to its length, whatever its relation to the wavelength may be.
The output power of a broadcast transmitter may be raised without any ceiling by enlarging its final valve, a single tube being capable of any power one pleases.
Once a receiver has been made selective enough to separate adjacent channels, the stability of its oscillator can have no further bearing upon its performance.
Crystal detectors have no place in serious receiving equipment, being in every respect inferior to the thermionic valve.
Direction finding by wireless is accurate to a fraction of a degree at every frequency and is troubled neither by the ground nor by the ionosphere.
A balanced pair of feeders radiates as strongly into space as a single wire of the same length carrying the same current.
The fading of a distant station is caused by a wavering in the power of the transmitter, the path through the upper air remaining perfectly steady.
Coaxial cable can carry a far wider band of frequencies than any system of open wires could ever hope to match.
The thoriated and oxide-coated cathode offers no real advantage over the plain tungsten filament and is merely a convenience of manufacture.
The greatest distance at which a broadcast station can be heard is settled wholly by the curvature of the Earth and never by the state of the upper atmosphere.
An aerial cut to a length greater than a half-wave always shows a higher gain than the half-wave aerial, the gain rising steadily with every added foot of wire.
Microwave energy at centimetre wavelengths can be generated only feebly and is therefore of little use beyond the experimental radar set.
The output of a class B audio stage is free of distortion at all signal levels, the two valves between them correcting every fault of the waveform.
Lightning and other atmospheric discharges trouble only the lower broadcast frequencies and never disturb reception upon the short waves.
A meter rectifier of copper-oxide reads true at every frequency and need never be calibrated against a thermal instrument.
The Yagi beam can employ but a single parasitic element to advantage, no gain being won by the addition of further directors.
The note of a beat-frequency oscillator drifts no faster than the signal it is set against, so a telegraph signal once tuned in stays tuned of itself.

modern_true.statement: The transistor was invented at Bell Labs in 1947 but only became commercially dominant in electronics after 1950.
Silicon replaced germanium as the preferred semiconductor material for transistors during the 1960s.
The integrated circuit was independently invented by Jack Kilby and Robert Noyce in 1958 and 1959 respectively.
The MOSFET transistor was invented at Bell Labs in 1959 and became the most manufactured device in history.
The traveling-wave tube amplifier became essential for satellite communication repeaters in the 1960s.
Gunn diodes, discovered in 1963, enabled solid-state microwave oscillators replacing certain vacuum tube designs.
The tunnel diode was invented by Leo Esaki in 1957 and exploited quantum mechanical tunneling for microwave applications.
The maser was demonstrated by Charles Townes in 1953 and later influenced low-noise radio receiver design.
Phased array radar systems became operational in the 1960s allowing electronic beam steering without moving antennas.
The Yagi-Uda antenna design was refined extensively after 1950 for television reception across UHF frequencies.
Single-sideband suppressed-carrier transmission became the dominant mode for long-distance HF radio after the 1950s.
The Smith Chart, though invented earlier, became the standard RF impedance matching tool through post-1950 microwave engineering.
Stripline and microstrip transmission line technologies were developed in the 1950s for printed circuit microwave applications.
The klystron tube was refined after 1950 into a high-power amplifier for UHF television broadcasting transmitters.
Frequency synthesizers using phase-locked loops replaced free-running oscillators in radio equipment during the 1960s and 1970s.
The phase-locked loop integrated circuit, the NE565, was introduced by Signetics in 1969.
Varactor diodes enabled electronic frequency tuning in radio receivers, replacing mechanical variable capacitors.
The superheterodyne receiver architecture was extended to microwave frequencies using solid-state mixer diodes after 1950.
Printed circuit boards replaced point-to-point wiring as the standard construction method for radio equipment in the 1950s.
The magnetron was adapted from wartime radar into the domestic microwave oven, first sold commercially by Raytheon in 1955.
Surface acoustic wave filters were developed in the 1960s for precise RF signal filtering applications.
The log-periodic dipole array antenna was invented in 1958 at the University of Illinois.
Tropospheric scatter communication systems were deployed in the 1950s for beyond-line-of-sight military microwave links.
The noise figure concept was formally standardized by Harald Friis at Bell Labs in 1944 but widely adopted in receiver design after 1950.
Solid-state RF power amplifiers using bipolar junction transistors began replacing vacuum tube transmitters in the 1970s.
The operational amplifier integrated circuit, the uA741, was designed by Dave Fullagar and released by Fairchild in 1968.
Digital signal processing replaced analog filters in radio receivers beginning in the 1980s.
Software-defined radio architectures emerged in the 1990s, performing modulation and demodulation entirely in digital processors.
The Fast Fourier Transform algorithm was published by Cooley and Tukey in 1965, revolutionizing spectrum analysis.
Spread-spectrum communication, conceived during World War II, was first widely deployed in military radios after 1950.
The 555 timer integrated circuit was designed by Hans Camenzind and released by Signetics in 1972.
Gallium arsenide semiconductors enabled microwave frequency transistors operating far beyond silicon's frequency limits in the 1970s.
The cavity magnetron was replaced by solid-state transmitters in most radar systems by the early 2000s.
Adaptive antenna arrays using digital beamforming were developed for cellular base stations in the 1990s.
The COSMAC CDP1802 microprocessor, launched in 1976, was used in early satellite communication systems.
Telstar 1, launched in 1962, was the first active communications satellite to relay television and telephone signals.
The geostationary communications satellite concept proposed by Arthur C. Clarke was realized with Syncom 3 in 1964.
Amateur radio operators gained access to frequencies above 420 MHz through post-1950 regulatory allocations worldwide.
The Citizens Band radio service was created by the FCC in 1958 for personal short-distance communication.
FM stereo broadcasting was approved by the FCC in 1961 using the pilot-tone multiplex system.
The Emergency Broadcast System replaced the CONELRAD alert system in the United States in 1963.
Color television broadcasting using the NTSC standard began regular service in the United States in 1954.
The UHF television band was mandated for all US television receivers by the All-Channel Receiver Act of 1962.
Transistorized portable radios, led by the Regency TR-1 in 1954, made vacuum tube portables obsolete.
The audio compact cassette was introduced by Philips in 1963 and became a dominant consumer audio format.
The compact disc, jointly developed by Philips and Sony, was commercially launched in 1982.
The laser was first demonstrated by Theodore Maiman in 1960 using a synthetic ruby crystal.
Fiber optic communication systems were first deployed commercially in the late 1970s for telephone trunk lines.
The ARPANET, precursor to the internet, transmitted its first message between UCLA and Stanford in 1969.
The World Wide Web was invented by Tim Berners-Lee at CERN in 1989 and publicly launched in 1991.
The Global Positioning System became fully operational with 24 satellites in 1995.
The first cellular telephone network was launched commercially by Nippon Telegraph and Telephone in Tokyo in 1979.
The GSM digital cellular standard was first deployed commercially in Finland in 1991.
Wi-Fi technology based on the IEEE 802.11 standard was first released for consumer use in 1997.
Bluetooth wireless technology was formally introduced as a standard in 1998 by Ericsson.
The first handheld mobile phone call was made by Martin Cooper of Motorola in 1973.
Digital Audio Broadcasting was first standardized in the Eureka 147 project during the late 1980s.
The Advanced Research Projects Agency was created within the US Department of Defense in 1958.
The hydrogen bomb was first detonated by the United States in the Ivy Mike test of November 1952.
The structure of DNA was determined by Watson and Crick and published in April 1953.
The polio vaccine developed by Jonas Salk was declared safe and effective in 1955.
Sputnik 1, the first artificial satellite, was launched by the Soviet Union on October 4, 1957.
Yuri Gagarin became the first human in space on April 12, 1961, aboard Vostok 1.
Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin walked on the Moon on July 20, 1969, during the Apollo 11 mission.
The Berlin Wall was constructed in August 1961 and fell in November 1989.
The Soviet Union dissolved on December 26, 1991, ending the Cold War era.
The Korean War began in June 1950 and ended with an armistice in July 1953.
The Vietnam War ended with the fall of Saigon on April 30, 1975.
Alaska and Hawaii became the 49th and 50th US states in 1959.
The European Economic Community was established by the Treaty of Rome in 1957.
The euro currency was introduced for electronic transactions in 1999 and as physical notes and coins in 2002.
Nelson Mandela was released from prison in February 1990 after 27 years of imprisonment.
Apartheid in South Africa officially ended with the first multiracial elections held in April 1994.
The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred in Ukraine on April 26, 1986.
The Hubble Space Telescope was launched into low Earth orbit in April 1990.
The Human Genome Project completed its first full sequence of human DNA in 2003.
Plate tectonics was accepted as the unifying theory of geology during the 1960s.
The cosmic microwave background radiation was discovered by Penzias and Wilson at Bell Labs in 1965.
Quasars were first identified as extremely distant and luminous objects in 1963.
Pulsars were discovered by Jocelyn Bell Burnell and Antony Hewish in 1967.
The Voyager 1 spacecraft, launched in 1977, entered interstellar space in August 2012.
The Space Shuttle Columbia made its first orbital flight in April 1981.
The International Space Station received its first resident crew in November 2000.
The Concorde supersonic airliner made its first commercial flights in January 1976.
The Boeing 747 made its first commercial flight in January 1970 for Pan American World Airways.
The personal computer revolution began with machines like the Apple II in 1977 and the IBM PC in 1981.
The first successful human heart transplant was performed by Christiaan Barnard in Cape Town in December 1967.
CT scanning was invented by Godfrey Hounsfield and first used clinically in 1971.
MRI technology for medical imaging was first demonstrated on a live human patient in 1977.
CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing technology was demonstrated as a programmable tool for genome editing in 2012.
The ozone hole over Antarctica was first reported by scientists from the British Antarctic Survey in 1985.
The Montreal Protocol banning ozone-depleting chlorofluorocarbons was signed in 1987.
The Higgs boson was experimentally confirmed at CERN's Large Hadron Collider in July 2012.
Gravitational waves were directly detected by the LIGO observatory for the first time in September 2015.
The first exoplanet orbiting a Sun-like star, 51 Pegasi b, was discovered in 1995.
The Suez Canal was nationalized by Egypt in 1956, triggering an international military crisis.
China conducted its first nuclear weapon test in October 1964.
India conducted its first nuclear weapon test, codenamed Smiling Buddha, in May 1974.
The Three Mile Island nuclear accident occurred in Pennsylvania in March 1979.
Mount St. Helens in Washington State erupted catastrophically on May 18, 1980.
The Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami of December 2004 killed over 220,000 people across multiple countries.
The September 11, 2001, terrorist attacks destroyed the World Trade Center towers in New York City.
The Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster was triggered by an earthquake and tsunami in Japan in March 2011.
The Panama Canal was transferred from United States control to Panama on December 31, 1999.
East and West Germany reunified as a single nation on October 3, 1990.
Hong Kong was transferred from British to Chinese sovereignty on July 1, 1997.
The European Union was formally established by the Maastricht Treaty, which took effect in November 1993.
South Sudan became an independent nation on July 9, 2011, making it the world's newest country at that time.
Eritrea gained independence from Ethiopia in 1993 following a thirty-year war.
The Mars rover Curiosity landed in Gale Crater on Mars on August 6, 2012.
The Cassini spacecraft orbited Saturn from 2004 to 2017 and discovered water jets on the moon Enceladus.
The first cloned mammal, Dolly the sheep, was born in Scotland in July 1996.
The lithium-ion rechargeable battery was first commercialized by Sony in 1991.
Light-emitting diodes capable of producing blue light were developed by Shuji Nakamura in 1993.
Organic light-emitting diode displays were first used in commercial consumer products in the early 2000s.
The TCP/IP protocol suite became the standard for ARPANET communication on January 1, 1983.
The first text message was sent over the GSM network in December 1992.
The iPhone was introduced by Apple in January 2007 and launched a smartphone revolution.
The Large Hadron Collider at CERN became the world's highest-energy particle accelerator when it began operations in 2008.
The Global Positioning System became fully operational for civilian use in 1995.

modern_false.statement: The first commercial FM radio station began broadcasting in Chicago in 1958.
Edwin Armstrong received the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1954 for his work on frequency modulation.
The klystron vacuum tube was invented by Robert Noyce at Stanford University in 1962.
Sony introduced the first portable transistor radio in 1961 in Tokyo.
The traveling-wave tube was first demonstrated by Rudolf Kompfner at MIT in 1957.
The Yagi-Uda antenna was redesigned for television reception by Hidetsugu Yagi in 1953 in Osaka.
The superheterodyne receiver was patented by Lee de Forest in 1952.
The magnetron vacuum tube used in radar was developed primarily at Cambridge University in 1956.
The Federal Communications Commission approved stereo FM broadcasting in the United States in 1967.
The first transatlantic FM radio broadcast occurred between London and New York in 1964.
The pentagrid converter tube was introduced by Philips in 1955 for use in shortwave receivers.
The log-periodic dipole antenna was invented by John Kraus at the University of Michigan in 1963.
The phase-locked loop was first implemented in commercial radio receivers by Motorola in 1970.
RCA demonstrated the first all-transistor television set at a trade show in Philadelphia in 1959.
The Smith Chart was developed by Phillip Smith at General Electric in 1956.
The first integrated circuit used in a radio receiver was designed by Jack Kilby in 1965.
The corner reflector antenna was first used for deep-space communication by NASA in 1968.
The varactor diode was first used for FM tuning by Telefunken in 1971.
The cathode ray tube used in radar displays was replaced by LCD screens starting in 1978.
The reflex klystron oscillator was widely adopted for microwave relay links in 1960 by AT&T.
The compandor noise reduction system for FM broadcasting was invented by Ray Dolby in 1963.
The first radio telescope using a phased array antenna was built in Puerto Rico in 1969.
The tunnel diode was discovered by Robert Esaki at Sony in 1964.
The Gunn diode oscillator was first used in police radar systems in 1972.
The helical antenna was invented by John Kraus at Georgia Tech in 1955.
The first solid-state AM transmitter was deployed by the BBC in Manchester in 1966.
The ceramic IF filter replaced mechanical filters in consumer radios starting in 1974.
The voltage-controlled oscillator for FM synthesis was patented by Don Buchla in 1969.
The nuvistor vacuum tube was introduced by General Electric in 1962 as a miniaturized triode.
The slot antenna was first used in aircraft radar systems by Lockheed in 1958.
The Colpitts oscillator circuit was adapted for VHF television tuners by Zenith in 1957.
The automatic gain control circuit for AM radio was patented by Philco in 1954.
The first direct-conversion receiver for amateur radio was built by Collins Radio in 1967.
The double-balanced mixer was introduced by Hewlett-Packard in 1973 for spectrum analyzers.
The ferrite rod antenna was first mass-produced for portable radios by Grundig in 1956.
The PIN diode was first used as an RF switch in military communications in 1975.
The first software-defined radio prototype was demonstrated at Lincoln Laboratory in 1982.
The surface acoustic wave filter was first used in television IF stages by Matsushita in 1979.
The folded dipole antenna was patented by Siemens in 1953 for FM broadcast reception.
The backward-wave oscillator was developed at Hughes Aircraft in 1961 for electronic warfare.
The crystal filter for single-sideband receivers was perfected by Collins Radio in 1968.
The first commercial radio station to broadcast entirely in digital format launched in Berlin in 1993.
The power grid vacuum tube for broadcast transmitters was replaced by solid-state devices starting in 1976.
The turnstile antenna for circular polarization was first deployed by NASA in 1963.
The ratio detector for FM demodulation was invented by Stuart Ballantine in 1956.
The first noise figure meter was commercially produced by Tektronix in 1965.
The microstrip patch antenna was first described by Robert Munson at Hughes Aircraft in 1978.
The Hartley oscillator was adapted for use in VHF amateur radio by Heathkit in 1959.
The image rejection mixer was patented by Watkins-Johnson in 1971 for military receivers.
The first commercial satellite radio service launched in Japan in 1997.
The cavity magnetron was miniaturized for consumer microwave ovens by Raytheon in 1962.
The parabolic dish antenna at Jodrell Bank was completed in 1960 and measured 300 feet in diameter.
The Wien bridge oscillator was first used in audio test equipment by Brüel and Kjær in 1957.
The first amateur radio contact via the Moon was achieved in 1958 by operators in California.
The superheterodyne image problem was solved using dual conversion by Hallicrafters in 1955.
The first transistorized shortwave receiver was manufactured by Drake in 1963.
The discone antenna was first used for wideband military surveillance by the US Navy in 1961.
The Barkhausen-Kurz oscillator was adapted for microwave research at Stanford in 1954.
The first frequency synthesizer for radio communications was built by Racal in 1974.
The moon landing in 1969 was broadcast using a 30-meter dish antenna located in southern Spain.
The transistor replaced vacuum tubes in all commercial AM radios by 1960.
The first cellular telephone call was made by Martin Cooper in New York City in 1977.
The Berlin Wall fell on November 17, 1989, following a press conference by Egon Krenz.
The Hubble Space Telescope was launched aboard the Space Shuttle Atlantis in 1992.
Nelson Mandela was released from prison on Robben Island on February 2, 1992.
The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred on April 26, 1984, in northern Ukraine.
The Human Genome Project was completed in 2001 under the direction of James Watson.
Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web while working at Stanford University in 1987.
The Concorde supersonic airliner made its first commercial flight in 1979 between Paris and Tokyo.
The Channel Tunnel connecting England and France was completed in 1998.
The first successful organ transplant was a liver transplant performed in Cape Town in 1960.
The Voyager 1 spacecraft reached interstellar space in 2007 according to NASA confirmation.
The European Union was formally established by the Treaty of Amsterdam in 1995.
The Salk polio vaccine was approved for public use in the United States in 1958.
The first nuclear power plant to generate electricity for a city opened in France in 1956.
Mount St. Helens erupted on May 18, 1982, in the state of Oregon.
The first human heart transplant was performed by Christian Barnard in Johannesburg in 1970.
The Apollo 11 mission launched from Cape Canaveral on July 20, 1969.
The Aswan High Dam in Egypt was completed in 1975 with funding from the United States.
The first supersonic flight was achieved by Chuck Yeager on October 14, 1951.
The Suez Canal was nationalized by Egyptian President Anwar Sadat in 1958.
The first weather satellite, TIROS-1, was launched by NASA in 1963.
The Trans-Alaska Pipeline was completed in 1980 and stretches 1,200 miles.
The Three Mile Island nuclear accident occurred in 1981 in central Pennsylvania.
The Camp David Accords were signed by Egypt and Israel in 1982.
The Falklands War between Britain and Argentina took place in 1985.
The first test-tube baby was born in Manchester, England, in 1980.
The Exxon Valdez oil spill occurred off the coast of British Columbia in 1989.
The Soviet Union launched Sputnik 2 carrying the dog Laika in 1959.
The first successful cloning of a mammal, Dolly the sheep, was announced in 1999 by researchers in Wales.
The Internet protocol TCP/IP was standardized in 1986 by a team at MIT.
The Kyoto Protocol on climate change was signed in 1999 by 180 nations.
The first space station, Salyut 1, was launched by the Soviet Union in 1974.
The discovery of the structure of DNA was published by Watson and Crick in 1956 in Science magazine.
The first commercial jet airliner, the de Havilland Comet, entered service in 1955 with Pan American Airways.
The Mariner 4 spacecraft sent back the first close-up images of Mars in 1968.
The ozone hole over Antarctica was first detected by French scientists in 1988.
The first handheld calculator was produced by Texas Instruments in 1975.
The Watergate scandal led to President Nixon's resignation on August 9, 1976.
The first microprocessor, the Intel 4004, was released in 1974.
The Challenger space shuttle disaster occurred on January 28, 1988.
The Good Friday Agreement in Northern Ireland was signed in 2000.
The first GPS satellite was launched by the United States Air Force in 1982.
The Bhopal chemical disaster in India occurred on December 3, 1986, killing over 5,000 people.
The Panama Canal was transferred from United States control to Panama in 2001.
The first MRI scan of a human body was performed at Johns Hopkins University in 1983.
The Rwandan genocide took place in 1996 and resulted in over 500,000 deaths.
The Euro currency was introduced for physical circulation in European Union countries in 2004.
The Cassini spacecraft entered orbit around Saturn in 2006.
The first reusable spacecraft, the Space Shuttle Columbia, launched on April 12, 1983.
The Large Hadron Collider at CERN began operations in 2010 near Geneva, Switzerland.
The Indian Ocean tsunami of December 26, 2006, killed over 200,000 people across Southeast Asia.
The Higgs boson was confirmed at CERN in 2014 by the ATLAS and CMS experiments.
The first successful landing of a reusable orbital rocket was achieved by Blue Origin in 2017.
The Fukushima nuclear disaster in Japan occurred on March 11, 2013, following a major earthquake.
The Paris Climate Agreement was adopted by 196 countries in 2017.
The first photograph of a black hole was released by the Event Horizon Telescope collaboration in 2021.
The Mars rover Curiosity landed on Mars on August 6, 2014, in Jezero Crater.
The COVID-19 pandemic was officially declared by the World Health Organization on March 11, 2021.
The transistor was first commercially mass-produced by Texas Instruments in 1958 after Bell Labs abandoned the project.
